title: Auction House: The Practical Operator Guide
author: Veltrix
excerpt: The Veltrix configuration decisions around economy that most operators get wrong and the structured approach that gets them right.

content.text: The insight quickly becomes apparent when operators try to sync the server: their Auction House Pro configuration stops working only after gameplay launches, not during initial setup. Upon deployment, Veltrix files freeze mid‑generation; every new trade and sale is duplicated, harming the economy. The root of the issue lies in the way the economy.yml and trade-rules.yml are parsed during the first tick. Operators discover that a stray comma or misplaced dash in the YAML causes the parser to drop the entire block, leaving default earn rates unchanged while the shop-items.yml remains untouched. This subtle syntactic break illustrates why operators postpone the deep dive into the configuration until the server is live, when they witness the immediate duplication and loss of revenue that can cripple a thriving market.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For

When operators search the community forums for fixes, they consistently encounter topics that mirror their own stumbling blocks: misaligned fee percentages in trade-rules.yml, missing price‑multiplier keys in market-config.yml, and improper list syntax in shop-items.yml that prevents the Auction House from indexing new items. The pattern shows that many servers struggle with the delicate interplay between the dynamic pricing engine and the static YAML entries. Operators often reply that they have already adjusted the earn rate in economy.yml, but the market fee still applies at the old rate because the refactor needs an explicit `refresh=true` flag. Questions like “Why does my created listing never appear in the GUI?” or “How do I set a custom tax rate that only applies to buyers?” point to a lack of understanding of nested YAML anchors and the way Veltrix merges default values at runtime. The recurring “I can’t see my listings” grievance implies that they are not applying the new `Auction House Pro` format, missing the mandatory `v0_1` key block structure. Consequently, they become trapped in a loop of editing files until the server restarts, unaware that the issue lies in the YAML precedence rules established by Veltrix. Only by revisiting the documentation for the `vx-eco-03` schema do they see that all vendor items must be declared under a `products:` mapping, and that the market fee must be expressed as a decimal rather than a percentage string. Once that nuance is cleared up, the auction system stabilises, and operators regain confidence in a predictable economy.
The Configuration Layer That Controls Market Behavior

On a Veltrix‑managed Hytale server the auction house behavior is dictated by the rows and columns in market‑config.yml. The list of parameters such as listing‑fee‑per‑click, fee‑multiplier‑sell, minimum‑bid‑step and auto‑expiry days give the operator fine‑grained control over how quickly items circulate and how costly it is to trade. A high listing fee of 5,000 roubles forces players to bide their time, while a fee multiplier of 1.3 at sell height compresses the profit margin when items move from high to low demand zones. Setting the minimum‑bid‑step to 100 roubles can eliminate micro‑flipping, but may also discourage new players from entering the market, causing a stagnant player‑bank balance. Auto‑expiry of 48 hours means wildly undervalued items get reclaimed and sent back to stock, keeping the market vibrant and preventing hoarding. Player behavior shifts along these levers: a generous fee environment encourages rapid buying and selling while restrictive settings promote careful, long‑term speculation.
Parameter Decisions That Determine Long-Term Economy Health

Operators should monitor the circulation rate; if items stay on the board for too long, they may indicate a low fee or high minimum bid, leading to inflated prices and dwindling liquidity. Conversely, too aggressive auctions that remove items too quickly can encourage price volatility but also foster opportunistic resellers who destabilise niche markets.
Converse, too aggressive auctions that remove items too quickly can encourage price volatility but also foster opportunistic resellers who destabilise niche markets.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

One of the most frequent hiccups that operators stumble over in Veltrix is setting economy.yml's earn_rate too high. When earn_rate is left at a flood value, coins accumulate faster than the market can absorb, triggering runaway inflation and driving item prices sideways. Many neglect to fine‑tune currency_generation_throttle, which allows a single economy tick to spawn triple the intended amount, and players quickly flood the shop. Another common error appears in trade‑rules.yml: an overly generous fee_percentage of 20% or more makes trading uninteresting and can push players to stop using the marketplace entirely, diluting the trade volume. Similarly, when market‑config.yml's price_multiplier is set to 0.8, items appear underpriced, causing a paradoxical surge of listings but a collapse in average transaction value. Finally, shop‑items.yml prices that are too low for inter‑guild supply chains can create an arms race of cheap resellers, matching the suspicion that operators forgot to audit old drop tables. All of these leaky configurations create friction and fear in the economy, pushing players to band together and seek alternative economies or botting, thereby undermining the server’s stability.
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

Operators often misconfigure Veltrix by leaving economy.yml's earn_rate too high. The resulting flood of coins per tick fuels runaway inflation, driving item prices sideways and eroding purchasing power. Neglecting currency_generation_throttle compounds this, causing the system to produce triple the intended amount in a single economy tick. A frequent second mistake is setting trade‑rules.yml fee_percentage to 20% or higher. Such heavy fees discourage trading, collapse marketplace liquidity, and push players to divest from the official market. Setting market‑config.yml price_multiplier too low, for example 0.8, underprices items, triggering a paradoxical surge in listings yet collapsing average transaction value. Finally, shop‑items.yml prices left below inter‑guild cost create a reselling arms race that any well‑informed player exploits. These misconfigurations create friction, low trust, and an overall unstable economy, compromising the server’s long‑term stability.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior
When player count exceeds 1000, the unbounded flat fee per listing can inflate the DB, causing slowdowns in market queries.

In Veltrix's economy.yml, the most impactful parameters are earn_rate, currency_gen_interval_seconds, trading_fee_percent, max_trades_per_day, market_listing_fee_amount, price_multiplier, and admin_shop_prices. Ordering by operational impact: earn_rate dictates how fast players accumulate funds; at low populations (≤50) a modest rate of 5.0 ensures moderate inflation, while at mid‑size clusters (200–500) boosting to 7.5 or 8.0 keeps rewards appealing. Next, currency_gen_interval_seconds controls the drop frequency; a 60‑second cycle works for small servers, but larger ones benefit from tightening to 30 seconds to smooth supply spikes. Trading_fee_percent shapes trade profitability; a 2% fee works for ≤200 players, scaling to 3–4% balances a 500‑player economy.
When Your Server Starts to Scale

Max_trades_per_day limits aggressive flipping; 50 trades per player is adequate for ≤200 users, but when you hit 700 players push to 100 to avoid spam. Market_listing_fee_amount is a flat charge for listing items; $5 for small groups, raising to $10 for thousands keeps the market treasury healthy. Price_multiplier spices price caps; a 1.0 multiplier is fine at 100 players, while 1.2 or higher helps tempers runaway pricing on larger stages. Finally, admin_shop_prices in shop-items.yml ensure high‑value items remain affordable; one should periodically re‑price based on average spend.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

The pre‑launch checklist for a Veltrix-powered Hytale server requires that every economic parameter in economy.yml be validated, ensuring the earn rate multiplier matches the intended player progression curve and that the currency generation cap is not saturated. The shop-items.yml file must be parsed so that all admin shop prices are within the economic balance, particularly verifying that the Auction House Pro VX‑ECO‑03 listing price is set to a realistic fee that does not disincentivize early traders. trade‑rules.yml should contain a trade fee percentage that is commensurate with the server’s inflation model, and an item transaction limit that protects against market manipulation. market‑config.yml must allocate a suitable listing fee and price multiplier for the new player market, while its cooldown period should be long enough to prevent rapid sell‑back cycles. All YAML files should pass syntax validation before deployment, and the compiled configuration must be tested in a sandbox environment, confirming that each fee, limit, and multiplier activates correctly at the first in‑world transaction. Finally, the economy.yml must reference a proper currency name that matches the assets listed in shop-items.yml to avoid currency mismatches during the auction process.

All YAML files must pass syntax validation before deployment and be sandbox‑tested, confirming every fee, limit, and multiplier activates at the first in‑world transaction. Finally, economy.yml must reference a currency name that matches assets in shop-items.yml, avoiding mismatches during the auction process.
The Veltrix Dynamic Pricing Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
